items.correct: The zigzag movement of sediment along a coast caused by waves approaching at an angle (swash up the beach) and gravity returning water perpendicular (backwash).
items.misconception: Sand moves along the beach because tides pull it sideways.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift is the rising of sea level over time.
items.why: Students confuse tides with waves. Longshore drift is a wave-driven process — the angle of swash vs straight-down backwash creates the zigzag, not tidal pull.

items.q: How do waves erode a cliff?
items.correct: Through hydraulic action (water compressing air in cracks), abrasion (waves throwing rocks against cliffs), attrition (rocks smashing against each other), and solution.
items.misconception: Waves erode cliffs by pushing water against the rock until it breaks.
items.random_wrong: Cliffs erode because of acid rain dissolving them.
items.why: Water pressure alone is rarely enough. The main mechanisms involve compressed air and rocks acting as tools — students underestimate how erosion needs material to do the wearing.

items.q: What happens in Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model?
items.correct: Death rates fall rapidly (due to better food, sanitation and medicine) while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth.
items.misconception: Birth rates rise rapidly because people want larger families.
items.random_wrong: Both birth and death rates fall sharply in Stage 2.
items.why: The population explosion in Stage 2 is driven by falling deaths, not rising births. Students see growth and assume more babies are being born when in fact more babies are surviving.

items.q: What is the dominant rock type along the Holderness coast?
items.why: Correct answer: Boulder clay. “Granite” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is an example of soft engineering?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Beach nourishment. “Sea wall” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Average rate of erosion at Holderness in metres per year?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
